QUESTION:
A short stay or 23-hour ward in a general and academic children's hospital: are they effective?

[0] We evaluated the usefulness of a short stay or 23-hour ward in a pediatric unit of a large teaching hospital, Westmead Hospital, and an academic Children's hospital, The New Children's Hospital, to determine if they are a useful addition to the emergency service.
[5] The short stay ward accounted for 10.3% (Westmead Hospital) and 14.7% (New Children's Hospital) of admissions, with 56% medical in nature, 30% surgical, and the remainder procedural or psychological.
[1] This is a descriptive comparison of prospectively collected data on all children admitted to the short stay ward at Westmead Hospital (WH) during 1994 and the short stay ward at the New Children's Hospital (NCH) during 1997-98.
[7] The short stay ward increased hospital efficiency with an average length of stay of 17.5 hours (Westmead Hospital) compared to 20.5 hours (New Children's Hospital).
[8] The users of the short stay ward were children of young age less than 2 years, with stay greater than 23 hours reported in only 1% of all admissions to the short stay ward.
[9] The rate of patient admission to an in-hospital bed was low, (4% [Westmead Hospital] compared to 6% [New Children's Hospital]), with the number of unscheduled visits within 72 hours of short stay ward discharge less than 1%.
[11] The short stay ward was developed through reallocation of resources from within the hospital to the short stay ward.
[6] Admission patterns were similar, with asthma, gastroenteritis, convulsion, pneumonia, and simple surgical conditions accounting for most short stay ward admissions.
[12] This resulted in estimated savings of $1/2 million (Westmead Hospital) to $2.3 million (New Children's Hospital) to the hospital, due to more efficient bed usage.
[3] The following outcome measures were used: length of stay, appropriateness of stay, rate of admission to an in-hospital bed, and rate of unscheduled visits within 72 hours of discharge.
[10] There were no adverse events reported at either short stay ward, with parental satisfaction high.
[4] Adverse events were reported and patient follow-up was attempted at 48 hours after discharge in all cases.
[2] These hospitals service an identical demographic area with the latter (NCH) a tertiary referral center.

Example ID: 11034241
QUESTION:
Is routine intraoperative frozen-section examination of sentinel lymph nodes in breast cancer worthwhile?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Routine intraoperative frozen section (FS) of sentinel lymph nodes (SLN) can detect metastatic disease, allowing immediate axillary dissection and avoiding the need for reoperation.
[3] We performed SLN biopsy with intraoperative FS in 890 consecutive breast cancer patients, none of whom had a back-up axillary dissection planned in advance.
[2] We examined the benefit of routine intraoperative FS among the first 1000 patients at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center who had SLN biopsy for breast cancer.
[1] Routine FS is also costly, increases operative time, and is subject to false-negative results.
[8] The benefit of FS in avoiding reoperative axillary dissection ranged from 4% for Tla (6 of 143) to 38% for T2 (45 of 119) cancers.
[5] The sensitivity of FS was determined as a function of (1) tumor size and (2) volume of metastatic disease in the SLN, and the benefit of FS was defined as the avoidance of a reoperative axillary dissection.
[7] The volume of SLN metastasis was highly correlated with tumor size, and FS was far more effective in detecting macrometastatic disease (sensitivity 92%) than micrometastases (sensitivity 17%).
[6] The sensitivity of FS ranged from 40% for patients with Tla to 76% for patients with T2 cancers.
[4] Serial sections and immunohistochemical staining for cytokeratins were performed on all SLN that proved negative on FS.

response: {"evidence": [{"sid": 0, "text": "Routine intraoperative frozen section (FS) of sentinel lymph nodes (SLN) can detect metastatic disease, allowing immediate axillary dissection and avoiding the need for reoperation.", "polarity": "support"}, {"sid": 1, "text": "Routine FS is also costly, increases operative time, and is subject to false-negative results.", "polarity": "oppose"}, {"sid": 6, "text": "The sensitivity of FS ranged from 40% for patients with Tla to 76% for patients with T2 cancers.", "polarity": "unclear"}, {"sid": 7, "text": "FS was far more effective in detecting macrometastatic disease (sensitivity 92%) than micrometastases (sensitivity 17%).", "polarity": "unclear"}, {"sid": 8, "text": "The benefit of FS in avoiding reoperative axillary dissection ranged from 4% for Tla (6 of 143) to 38% for T2 (45 of 119) cancers.", "polarity": "support"}], "reasoning_steps": ["Define ‘worthwhile’ as balance between benefit (avoiding reoperation) and harms (cost, time, false negatives).", "FS avoids reoperative axillary dissection in a minority overall, with benefit varying by tumor size (4–38%).", "FS has limited sensitivity overall and performs poorly for micrometastases, so many positives are still missed.", "FS adds cost and operative time, so routine use for all patients may not be justified; benefit is greater in higher-stage tumors."], "counterpoints": ["High sensitivity for macrometastases suggests FS may be worthwhile in selected higher-risk patients.", "Even a modest reduction in reoperations may be valued in some settings despite cost and false negatives."], "uncertainty_flags": ["Threshold for ‘worthwhile’ depends on local costs, patient preferences, and reoperation burden.", "Data are from a single-center cohort and may not generalize to all practice settings."], "confidence": 0.78}

QUESTION:

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] The combined use of free and total prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in early detection of prostate cancer has been controversial.
[1] This article systematically evaluates the discriminating capacity of a large number of combination tests.

[6] The average area under the receiver operating characteristic curve across test data sets was 0.74 for total PSA and 0.76 for the combination tests.

[5] For robustness, we considered 35 test-train splits of the original data and computed receiver operating characteristic curves for each test data set.
[3] We used a classification algorithm called logic regression to search for clinically useful tests combining total and percent free PSA and receiver operating characteristic analysis and compared these tests with those based on total and complexed PSA.
[8] All these tests extended the PSA reflex range to below 4.0 ng/mL.
[9] Receiver operating characteristic curve analysis indicated that the overall diagnostic performance as expressed by the area under the curve did not differ significantly for the different tests.
[4] Data were divided into training and test subsets.

Example ID: 16100194
QUESTION:
Are physicians aware of the side effects of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACE-I) are considered safe, but they are associated with characteristic side effects, namely cough and angioedema, usually requiring discontinuation.
[3] A questionnaire (13 questions) on side effects of ACE-I was posted to physicians.
[10] Cardiologists seemed to be less aware of the fact that angiotensin receptor blockers (sartans) can cross-react with ACE-I.
[6] Of the 154 physicians (mean age, 45 years) 48 were cardiologists, 52 were GPs, and 54 were allergists.
[1] We perceived that referrals for these side effects have become more and more frequent;
[2] therefore, we evaluated the degree of knowledge on the safety of ACE-I in different medical categories.
[7] The percentage of correct answers was low: 31.9% for cardiologists, 40% for GPs, and 33% for allergists.
[9] The lower rate of correct answers (0 to 15.9%) concerned the time of onset of cough and the action to take.
[8] Thus, GPs provided a significantly higher percentage of correct answers with respect to the remaining categories (p = 0.05).
[4] Cardiologists, allergists, and general practitioners (GPs) from the National Healthcare System.
[5] Three hundred twelve physicians were contacted, and 154 returned questionnaires that could be analyzed.

Example ID: 16253970
QUESTION:
Is controlled ovarian stimulation in intrauterine insemination an acceptable therapy in couples with unexplained non-conception in the perspective of multiple pregnancies?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) with intrauterine insemination (IUI) is a common treatment in couples with unexplained non-conception.
[3] A retrospective cohort study was performed in all couples with unexplained non-conception undergoing COS-IUI in the Academic Hospital of Maastricht.
[1] Induction of multifollicular growth is considered to improve pregnancy outcome, but it contains an increased risk of multiple pregnancies and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.
[2] In this study the impact of the number of follicles (>14 mm) on the ongoing pregnancy rate (PR) and multiple PR was evaluated in the first four treatment cycles.
[8] Ongoing PR was highest in the first treatment cycle and declined significantly with increasing cycle order (P=0.006), while multiple PR did not change.
[7] No significant difference was found in ongoing PR between women with one, two, three or four follicles respectively (P=0.54), but in women with two or more follicles 12/73 pregnancies were multiples.
[5] Secondary outcomes were ongoing multiple PR, number of follicles of>or=14 mm, and order of treatment cycle.
[6] Three hundred couples were included.
[4] The main outcome measure was ongoing PR.

[1] One hundred fifty patients with primary prostate cancer (T(1-3)N(0)M(0)) were informed by means of a decision aid of two treatment options: radiotherapy with 70 Gy versus 74 Gy.
[2] The latter treatment is associated with more cure and more toxicity.
[8] The lower dose was chosen more often by the older patients, low-risk patients, patients without hormone treatment, and patients with a low anxiety or depression score.
[5] Patients who wanted to choose their own treatment (n = 119) are described here.
[3] The patients were asked whether they wanted to choose, and if so which treatment they preferred.
[6] The majority of these patients (75%) chose the lower radiation dose.

[4] They also assigned importance weights to the probability of various outcomes, such as survival, cure and adverse effects.

Example ID: 17051586
QUESTION:
Can folic acid protect against congenital heart defects in Down syndrome?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Several studies have suggested a protective effect of folic acid (FA) on congenital heart anomalies.
[1] Down syndrome (DS) infants are known to have a high frequency of heart anomalies.
[3] Our objectives were to investigate whether first-trimester FA use protects against heart anomalies among DS children.
[4] Women with liveborn DS children participating in the Slone Epidemiology Center Birth Defects Study between 1976 and 1997 were included.
[6] Subanalyses were performed for defects that have been associated with FA in non-DS populations (conotruncal, ventricular septal [VSD]) and for those that are associated with DS (ostium secundum type atrial septal defects [ASD]and endocardial cushion defects [ECD]).
[9] After adjustment for possible confounders, no protective effect of FA was found on heart anomalies overall (OR 0.95, 95% CI: 0.61-1.47) nor separately for conotruncal defects, VSDs, ASDs, or ECDs.
[5] We performed case-control analyses using DS, with heart anomalies as cases and DS, without heart anomalies as controls.
[2] Not all children with DS suffer from heart anomalies, which raises the question whether maternal factors might affect the risk of these anomalies.
[7] Exposure was defined as the use of any FA-containing product for an average of at least 4 days per week during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy, whereas no exposure was defined as no use of FA in these 12 weeks.
[8] Of the 223 cases, 110 (49%) were exposed versus 84 (46%) of the 184 controls.

Given a QUESTION and CANDIDATE SENTENCES (each with id), output helpful signals for decision.
Return ONLY a valid JSON object with keys:

  reasoning_steps: list of short strings (2~5 items)
  counterpoints: list of short strings (0~3 items)
  uncertainty_flags: list[str] (0~3)
  confidence: float 0.0~1.0
Constraints:
- evidence length <= 6
- each evidence text <= 240 chars
- each reasoning step <= 180 chars
- output JSON only, no extra text.

"text": "we achieved complete follow-up and classified 5-year breast cancer-specific prognosis among proband (mother or oldest sister) into tertiles as poor, intermediary, or good.", "polarity": "support"}, {"sid": 5, "text": "We used Kaplan-Meier estimates of survival proportions and Cox models to calculate relative risks of dying from breast cancer within 5 years depending on the proband's outcome.", "polarity": "support"}, {"sid": 6, "text": "The 5-year survival proportion among daughters whose mothers died within 5 years was 87% compared to 91% if the mother was alive (p = 0.03).", "polarity": "support"}, {"sid": 8, "text": "After adjustment for potential confounders, daughters and sisters of a proband with poor prognosis had a 60% higher 5-year breast cancer mortality compared "support"}], "reasoning_steps": ["The study examines whether relatives’ outcomes correlate with proband breast cancer prognosis, implying possible inherited prognostic factors.", "Prognosis of probands was categorized and relatives’ 5‑year breast cancer mortality was compared across these categories.", "Relatives of probands with poor prognosis had significantly higher 5‑year breast cancer mortality than relatives of probands with good prognosis, even after adjustment.", "These findings indicate that breast cancer prognosis shows familial aggregation, consistent with a heritable component to prognosis.", "Thus, while mechanisms are not fully defined, the data support that breast cancer prognosis is at least partly inherited."], "counterpoints": ["The text notes it was Example ID: 17691856
QUESTION:
Midwives' competence: is it affected by working in a rural location?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[5] Questionnaires were sent to 82 midwives working in remote and rural areas and 107 midwives working in urban hospitals with midwife-led units.
[2] A key consideration is that midwives have the skills to competently and confidently provide maternity services in rural areas, which may be geographically isolated and where the midwife may only see a small number of pregnant women each year.
[6] The response rate from midwives in rural settings was considerably higher (85%) than from midwives in the urban areas (60%).
[8] Midwives in the rural group were more likely to report competence for breech delivery (p = 0.001), while more urban midwives reported competence in skills such as intravenous fluid replacement (p<0.001) and initial and discharge examination of the newborn (p<0.001).
[11] Lack of time was a greater barrier for urban midwives (p = 0.02), whereas distance to training was greater for rural midwives (p = 0.009).
[4] This was a comparative questionnaire survey involving a stratified sample of remote and rural maternity units and an ad hoc comparison group of three urban maternity units in Scotland.
[3] Our objective was to compare the views of midwives in rural and urban settings, regarding their competence and confidence with respect to 'competencies' identified as being those which all professionals should have in order to provide effective and safe care for low-risk women.
[1] In the UK, the result has been that many rural maternity units have become midwife-led.
[7] Although the proportion of midwives who reported that they were competent was broadly similar in the two groups, there were some significant differences regarding specific competencies.
[12] Lack of motivation or interest was significantly higher in urban units (p = 0.006).
[0] Rising health care costs and the need to consolidate expertise in tertiary services have led to the centralisation of services.
[10] however, more of the rural group had attended an educational event within the last month (p<0.001).
[9] Both groups reported facing barriers to continuing professional development;

QUESTION:
Body diffusion-weighted MR imaging of uterine endometrial cancer: is it helpful in the detection of cancer in nonenhanced MR imaging?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] In this study, the authors discussed the feasibility and value of diffusion-weighted (DW) MR imaging in the detection of uterine endometrial cancer in addition to conventional nonenhanced MR images.

[6] In the remaining 4 patients, of whom two had coexisting leiomyoma, no cancer was detected on T2-weighted images.
[3] Moreover, the apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) values were measured in the regions of interest within the endometrial cancer and compared with those of normal endometrium and myometrium in 31 volunteers, leiomyoma in 14 patients and adenomyosis in 10 patients.
[5] In 19 of 23 patients, endometrial cancers were detected only on T2-weighted images.
[8] When DW images and fused DW images/T2-weighted images were used in addition to the T2-weighted images, cancers were identified in 3 of the remaining 4 patients in addition to the 19 patients (overall detection sensitivity of 96%).
[2] This study investigated whether or not DW images offer additional incremental value to conventional nonenhanced MR imaging in comparison with histopathological results.
[9] The mean ADC value of endometrial cancer (n=22) was (0.97+/-0.19)x10(-3)mm(2)/s, which was significantly lower than those of the normal endometrium, myometrium, leiomyoma and adenomyosis (p<0.05).
[7] This corresponds to an 83% detection sensitivity for the carcinomas.
[4] The Wilcoxon rank sum test was used, with a p<0.05 considered statistically significant.

QUESTION:
Does laparoscopic surgery decrease the risk of atrial fibrillation after foregut surgery?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[2] We examined the factors associated with new-onset atrial fibrillation after foregut surgery at our institution.

[9] Multivariate analysis revealed an association of atrial fibrillation with age (OR 1.08, 95% CI 1.02-1.14, p = 0.01), and laparoscopic surgery (OR 0.09, 95% CI 0.01-0.95, p = 0.04) after adjusting for surgery type.
[0] Atrial fibrillation, which occurs in 12% of all major foregut surgeries, can prolong hospital stay and increase morbidity.
[5] Of the 154 patients, 14 patients developed new-onset atrial fibrillation with a higher mean age of 67.1 years (+/-8.8 years) versus 56.4 years (+/-14.1 years) (p = 0.006).
[3] We retrospectively examined the records of 154 adult patients who underwent major foregut surgery which included esophagectomy, partial or total gastrectomy, redo Heller myotomy, redo or transthoracic fundoplications.
[4] Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analysis with standard modeling techniques were performed to determine risk factors for new-onset atrial fibrillation.
[1] Minimally invasive techniques in foregut surgery have been suggested to cause less tissue trauma.
[7] Patients with atrial fibrillation had received more fluid (6.5 +/- 2.8 liters versus 5.3 +/- 2.0 liters) and had longer operations (370 +/- 103 min versus 362 +/- 142 min), none of which were statistically significant.
[8] The average intensive care length of stay of patients was longer: 7.5 +/- 6.8 days versus 4.0 +/- 7.1 days (p = 0.004).

QUESTION:
Do general practice characteristics influence uptake of an information technology (IT) innovation in primary care?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Recent evaluations of IT innovations in primary care have highlighted variations between centres and practices in uptake and use.
[1] We evaluated whether structural characteristics of a general practice were associated with variations in use of a web-based clinical information system underpinning a Managed Clinical Network in diabetes, between the years 2001 and 2003.
[3] In regression analyses, we determined whether total use was associated with structural characteristics of the practice (total list size, training status, numbers of GPs (general practitioners), mean age of the GPs, numbers of female GPs, level of deprivation of the population and whether staff had received advanced training in diabetes care).
[8] In linear regression analyses, none of the general practice variables were associated with online use, either overall or stratified by staff type, except for the numbers of diabetes-educated staff.
[2] Using a computerised audit trail, we calculated the numbers of web-based operations that occurred in each practice, stratified by staff type and year, and adjusted for the numbers of registered diabetic patients.
[7] Frequent use by GPs was evident in only a small number of practices, with mean GP use decreasing over the three years.
[4] Initially there were a few practices which made very frequent use of the information system, with relatively high numbers of practices using the facility infrequently.
[5] However, overall use gradually became more evenly spread.
[9] This was consistently associated with increased use by nurses and GPs.
[6] This effect was particularly evident among nurse users.

Is the ability to perform transurethral resection of the prostate influenced by the surgeon's previous experience?

[3] a senior urologist with vast transurethral resection of the prostate experience (Group III - 19 patients).

[4] The following were recorded: the weight of resected tissue, the duration of the resection procedure, the volume of irrigation used, the amount of irrigation absorbed and the hemoglobin and sodium levels in the serum during the procedure.

[5] There were no differences between the groups in the amount of irrigation fluid used per operation, the amount of irrigation fluid absorbed or hematocrit and hemoglobin variation during the procedure.
[8] Four patients (6%) presented with TUR syndrome, without a significant difference between the groups.
[7] The mean absorbed irrigation fluid was similar between the groups, with no statistical difference between them (p=0.24).

Are octogenarians at high risk for carotid endarterectomy?

[5] Perioperative morbidity and mortality, including neurologic events and myocardial infarction, were recorded.
[10] There were 4 perioperative neurologic complications, which included 1 transient ischemic attack (0.97%), 2 minor strokes (1.94%), and 1 major stroke (0.97%).

[11] Group 1: 191 ureteroscopies, Group 2: 95 ureteroscopies.

Example ID: 21593045
QUESTION:
Do women with ovaries of polycystic morphology without any other features of PCOS benefit from short-term metformin co-treatment during IVF?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Women with ovaries of polycystic morphology (PCO), without any other features of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), respond similarly to women with PCOS when stimulated with exogenous gonadotrophins, and both groups share various endocrinological disturbances underlying their pathology.
[1] In women with PCOS, metformin co-treatment during IVF has been shown to increase pregnancy rates and reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS).
[2] The aim of this study was to investigate whether metformin co-treatment before and during IVF can also increase the live birth rate (LBR) and lower severe OHSS rates for women with PCO, but no other manifestations of PCOS.
[4] The study population included 134 women with ovulatory PCO (and no evidence of clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism) undergoing IVF treatment at three tertiary referral IVF units.
[7] With regard to IVF outcome, no significant improvements were found in the metformin group when compared with the placebo group.
[5] In total, 134 women were randomized, 69 to metformin and 65 to placebo.
[8] In particular, there was no difference between the groups in rates of live birth [metformin n = 27 (39.1%), placebo n = 30 (46.2), (95% confidence interval 0.38, 1.49, odds ratio = 0.75)], clinical pregnancy [metformin n = 29 (42.0%), placebo n = 33 (50.8%)]or severe OHSS [metformin n = 6 (8.7%), placebo n = 5 (7.7%)].
[6] There were no statistically significant differences between the two groups in baseline characteristics.
[3] This study was a double-blind, multi-centre, randomized, placebo-controlled trial.

Example ID: 21712147
QUESTION:
Does combining antiretroviral agents in a single dosage form enhance quality of life of HIV/AIDS patients?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Combining various antiretroviral agents into one single dosage form has been a strategy to reduce pill burden and enhance medication adherence among human immunodeficiency virus /AIDS (HIV/AIDS) patients.
[10] One hundred seventy HIV/AIDS patients with ≥2 antiretroviral agents were identified from the MEPS database, of which 53% (n=92) were on FXD formulation.
[2] The Medical Expenditure Panel Survey (MEPS) was used to identify HIV/AIDS patients with ≥2 active antiretroviral medications.
[1] This is a cost-utility study from a health care system's perspective comparing coformulated fixed dose (FXD) strategy versus multiple free dose combination (FRC) in antiretroviral therapy.
[11] On matching, 70 patients from FXD had a match from the FRC group.
[3] Patients on FXD were matched in 1:1 ratio with the FRC group using propensity scores.
[16] The ICUR for the FXD treatment over FRC treatment was $45,540.49/quality-adjusted life years (QALYs).
[8] Nine FXD antiretroviral formulations approved by the U.S.
[9] Food and Drug Administration by 2005 was included in this study.
[4] All medical costs excluding those paid by patients and families were included.
[7] A cost-effectiveness acceptability curve was determined using a Monte Carlo probabilistic simulation technique.
[12] No differences in sociodemographic and health status variables were observed between the matched groups.
[13] The mean annual cost was $15,766.15 for FXD patients and $11,875.21 for FRC patients.
[17] Probabilistic sensitivity analysis showed FXD to dominate FRC (>50% probability of being cost-effective) above the $40,000 threshold.
[14] The mean utility gained by using FXD over FRC was 0.085;
[15] however, this difference was not statistically significant.
[6] Incremental cost-utility ratios (ICURs) were calculated using the mean annual estimates.
[5] Utility was measured using SF-6D scores from the SF-12 questionnaire.

[5] At Y15, 45.1% of subjects had knee pain.

[2] Knee pain at Y15 was assessed by questionnaire.

[4] BMI significantly increased from Y1 to Y15 (P<0.0005) with medians (interquartile ranges) of 24.5 kg/m(2) (22.5-27.2 kg/m(2) ) and 26.5 kg/m(2) (23.9-30.1 kg/m(2) ), respectively.

Example ID: 21881325
QUESTION:
Do preoperative statins reduce atrial fibrillation after coronary artery bypass grafting?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[1] The objective of this study was to assess the efficacy of preoperative statin therapy in preventing AF after coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG).
[0] Recent studies have demonstrated that statins have pleiotropic effects, including anti-inflammatory effects and atrial fibrillation (AF) preventive effects.
[4] Patients were divided into two groups to examine the influence of statins: those with preoperative statin therapy (Statin group, n = 77) and those without it (Non-statin group, n = 126).
[9] Multivariate analysis demonstrated that independent predictors of AF development after CABG were preoperative statin therapy (odds ratio [OR]0.327, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.107 to 0.998, p = 0.05) and age (OR 1.058, 95% CI 1.004 to 1.116, p = 0.035).
[3] 14 patients with preoperative AF and 4 patients with concomitant valve surgery were excluded from this study.
[5] In addition, patients were divided into two groups to determine the independent predictors for postoperative AF: those with postoperative AF (AF group, n = 54) and those without it (Non-AF group, n = 149).
[8] Postoperative AF was significantly lower in the Statin group compared with the Non-statin group (16% versus 33%, p = 0.005).
[2] 221 patients underwent CABG in our hospital from 2004 to 2007.
[7] The overall incidence of postoperative AF was 26%.
[6] Patient data were collected and analyzed retrospectively.

Example ID: 21889895
QUESTION:
Will CT ordering practices change if we educate residents about the potential effects of radiation exposure?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] The aim of this study was to determine if educating residents about the potential effects of radiation exposure from computed tomographic (CT) imaging alters ordering patterns.
[2] Two to four months after a radiologist's lecture on the potential effects of radiation exposure related to CT scans, urology and orthopedic residents were surveyed regarding the number and types of CT scans they ordered, the use of alternative imaging modalities, and whether they used the lecture information to educate patients.
[7] Patients directed questions about radiation exposure to 57% (12 of 21);
[8] 38% (eight of 21) used the lecture information to educate patients.
[5] The types of CT scans ordered changed after the lecture for 14% (three of 21).
[4] The number of CT scans ordered after the lecture stayed constant for 90% (19 of 21) and decreased for 10% (two of 21).
[1] This study also explored whether referring physicians are interested in radiation education and was an initial effort to address their CT ordering behavior.
[9] Referring physicians were interested in the topic, and afterward, other physician groups requested radiation education lectures.
[6] Thirty-three percent (seven of 21) reported increases in alternative imaging after the lecture, including 24% (five of 21) reporting increases in magnetic resonance imaging and 19% (four of 21) reporting increases in ultrasound.
[3] Twenty-one resident lecture attendants completed the survey.

Example ID: 22449464
QUESTION:
Do general practice selection scores predict success at MRCGP?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Selection into general practice training is undertaken using a competency based approach.
[3] The MRCGP comprises of an applied knowledge test (AKT), a clinical skills assessment (CSA) and workplace-based assessments (WPBA).AIM: To explore the predictive validity of general practice selection scores using the AKT and CSA elements of the MRCGP as a final outcome measure.
[2] However, the relationship between selection and performance in the Royal College of General Practitioner examinations (MRCGP) has yet to be explored.
[6] Correlation was used to explore associations between selection scores and examination scores.
[5] Selection data consisted of an overall selection score as well as scores from each individual stage of selection.
[7] The score for overall performance at selection achieved statistically significant correlation with examination performance (r = 0.491 for the AKT and r = 0.526 for the CSA, P<0.01).
[4] This study carried out a retrospective analysis of 101 trainees from the Wales Deanery who were successfully selected on to general practice training in 2007.
[1] The clear advantage of this approach over traditional methods has been demonstrated through evaluation of its validity and reliability.

Example ID: 24487044
QUESTION:
Pharmacologic regimens for knee osteoarthritis prevention: can they be cost-effective?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] We sought to determine the target populations and drug efficacy, toxicity, cost, and initiation age thresholds under which a pharmacologic regimen for knee osteoarthritis (OA) prevention could be cost-effective.
[1] We used the Osteoarthritis Policy (OAPol) Model, a validated state-transition simulation model of knee OA, to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of using disease-modifying OA drugs (DMOADs) as prophylaxis for the disease.
[8] for the obese only and no risk factors cohorts, the base case DMOAD was less cost-effective than the standard of care.
[9] Regimens priced at $3,000 per year and higher demonstrated ICERs above cost-effectiveness thresholds consistent with current US standards.
[2] We assessed four cohorts at varying risk for developing OA: (1) no risk factors, (2) obese, (3) history of knee injury, and (4) high-risk (obese with history of knee injury).
[4] Outcomes included costs, quality-adjusted life expectancy (QALE), and incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs).
[7] ICERs>$150,000/QALY were observed when comparing the base case DMOAD to the standard of care in the knee injury only cohort;
[6] For the high-risk cohort, base case prophylaxis increased quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) by 0.04 and lifetime costs by $4,600, and produced an ICER of $118,000 per QALY gained.
[3] The base case DMOAD was initiated at age 50 with 40% efficacy in the first year, 5% failure per subsequent year, 0.22% major toxicity, and annual cost of $1,000.
[5] Key parameters were varied in sensitivity analyses.

Example ID: 24516646
QUESTION:
Is the determination of specific IgE against components using ISAC 112 a reproducible technique?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] The ImmunoCAP ISAC 112 is a fluoro-immunoassay that allows detection of specific IgE to 112 molecular components from 51 allergenic sources.
[5] To evaluate the accuracy of ISAC 112, we contrasted the determinations of CTR02 calibrator with their expected values by T Student test.
[1] We studied the reliability of this technique intra- and inter- assay, as well as inter-batch- and inter-laboratory-assay.
[4] Furthermore, all samples were tested in two laboratories and with two batches of ISAC kit.
[2] Twenty samples were studied, nineteen sera from polysensitized allergic patients, and the technique calibrator provided by the manufacturer (CTR02).
[8] The mean CV obtained in the CTR02 determinations was of 9.4%, and the variability of sera from patients was of 22.9%.
[11] In the inter-laboratory analyse, we obtained a very good agreement to 73 allergens, good to 22, moderate to 6 and poor to two allergens.
[10] In the inter-batch analyse, we obtained a very good agreement to 82 allergens, good to 14, moderate to 5 allergens, poor to one, and bad to 1 allergen.
[6] To analyse the precision, we calculated the coefficient of variation (CV) of the 15 allergens that generate the calibration curve, and to analyse the repeatability and the reproducibility, we calculated the intraclass coefficient correlation (ICC) to each allergen.
[7] The results obtained for CTR02 were similar to those expected in 7 of 15 allergens that generate the calibration curve, whereas in 8 allergens the results showed significant differences.
[9] The agreement in the intra- and inter-assay analysis was very good to 94 allergens and good to one.
[3] We measured the sIgE from CTR02 and three patients' sera ten times in the same and in different assays.

QUESTION:
May mean platelet volume levels be a predictor in the diagnosis of pelvic inflammatory disease?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Our aim in this study was to investigate whether mean platelet volume (MPV) value could be used as an early marker to predict pelvic inflammatory disease (PID).
[2] The control group consisted of 44 women who applied to the clinic for a routine gynaecological check-up, without chronic disease or a history of medication use.
[6] MPV values in patients with PID were lower than those in the control group.
[1] Overall, 44 patients with PID and 44 healthy women were included in the study.
[4] The leukocyte count, platelet count, neutrophil ratio and MPV values were collected from PID and the control group.
[8] A negative correlation was discovered between platelet count and MPV values (p = 0.019, r = - 0.425).
[7] This reduction in MPV is statistically significant when the PID patient group is compared with the control group (p < 0.001).
[3] Owing to the fact that it would affect thrombocyte function, women who have the following conditions were excluded from the study: women who were taking anticoagulant therapy, oral contraceptives, nonsteroid anti-inflammatory medications and who had chronic diseases.
[5] C reactive protein values of patients with PID were also noted.
[9] Receiver-operating curve analysis pointed out that MPV has greater area under curve value than neutrophil rate, leukocyte and platelet count (0.73, 0.64, 0.72 and 0.49 respectively).

Example ID: 25501465
QUESTION:
Evaluation of pediatric VCUG at an academic children's hospital: is the radiographic scout image necessary?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[2] To demonstrate that the radiographic scout image does not augment VCUG interpretation or contribute management-changing information but nonetheless carries a non-negligible effective dose.
[0] There is heterogeneity in how pediatric voiding cystourethrography (VCUG) is performed.
[1] Some institutions, including our own, obtain a radiographic scout image prior to contrast agent instillation.
[3] We evaluated 181 children who underwent VCUG in 2012, with an age breakdown of less than 1 year (56 children), 1-5 years (66 children), 6-10 years (43 children) and 11-18 years (16 children), with a mean age of 4.0 years.
[4] We investigated patient demographics, clinical indication for the examination, scout image findings and estimated effective radiation dose, as well as overall exam findings and impression.
[6] Scout image estimated effective radiation dose averaged 0.09 mSv in children younger than 1 y, 0.09 mSv in children age 1-5, 0.13 mSv in children age 6-10 and 0.18 mSv in children age 11-18.
[8] Evaluation of known or suspected vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) and urinary tract infection (UTI) were the most common clinical indications, stated in 40.9% and 37.0% of exams, respectively.
[7] Total fluoroscopy time per examination averaged 36.7 s (range 34.8-39.6 s for all age group averages).
[5] No clinically significant or management-changing findings were present on scout images, and no radiopaque urinary tract calculi or concerning incidental finding was identified.

Example ID: 25985014
QUESTION:
Is ganglionated plexi ablation during Maze IV procedure beneficial for postoperative long-term stable sinus rhythm?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] We investigated the role of surgical ablation targeting the autonomous nervous system during a Cox-Maze IV procedure in the maintenance of sinus rhythm at long-term follow-up.
[1] The patient population consisted of 519 subjects with persistent or long-standing persistent atrial fibrillation (AF) undergoing radiofrequency Maze IV during open heart surgery between January 2006 and July 2013 at three institutions without (Group 1) or with (Group 2) ganglionated plexi (GP) ablation.
[5] The percentage of patients in normal sinus rhythm (NSR) off-antiarrhythmic drugs did not differ between groups (Group 1-75.5%, Group 2-67.8%, p = 0.08).
[7] In contrast the absence of GP ablation was not a significant factor (p = 0.12).
[6] Duration of AF ≥ 38 months (p = 0.01), left atrial diameter ≥ 54 mm (0.001), left atrial area ≥ 33 cm(2) (p = 0.005), absence of connecting lesions (p= 0.04), and absence of right atrial ablation (p<0.001) were independently associated with high incidence of AF recurrence.
[4] Median follow-up was 36.7 months.
[3] Predictors of AF recurrence were evaluated by means of competing risk regression.
[2] Recurrence of atrial fibrillation off-antiarrhythmic drugs was the primary outcome.

[6] The total number of metastases was significantly higher on T1w than on STIR images at both field strengths (p<0.05).
[3] All patients who showed typical signs of bone metastases were included in the study.
[4] Evaluation of the images was performed by the calculation of the number of metastases by three independent readers and by visual assessment on a 4-point scale.
[2] The standard protocol comprised STIR and T1w sequences.
[5] 86 patients fulfilled the inclusion criteria.

Example ID: 26163474
QUESTION:
Is there a connection between sublingual varices and hypertension?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[10] An association between sublingual varices and hypertension was found (OR = 2.25, p<0.002).
[13] Sublingual varices indicate hypertension with a positive predictive value of 0.5 and a negative predictive value of 0.80.
[0] Sublingual varices have earlier been related to ageing, smoking and cardiovascular disease.
[1] The aim of this study was to investigate whether sublingual varices are related to presence of hypertension.
[3] Digital photographs of the lateral borders of the tongue for grading of sublingual varices were taken, and blood pressure was measured.
[11] Mean systolic blood pressure was 123 and 132 mmHg in patients with grade 0 and grade 1 sublingual varices, respectively (p<0.0001, CI 95 %).
[12] Mean diastolic blood pressure was 80 and 83 mmHg in patients with grade 0 and grade 1 sublingual varices, respectively (p<0.005, CI 95 %).
[4] Those patients without previous diagnosis of hypertension and with a noted blood pressure ≥ 140 mmHg and/or ≥ 90 mmHg at the dental clinic performed complementary home blood pressure during one week.
[7] Each photograph was graded as none/few (grade 0) or medium/severe (grade 1) presence of sublingual varices.
[9] Power calculation stipulated a study population of 323 patients.
[8] Pearson's Chi-square test, Student's t-test, and multiple regression analysis were applied.
[2] In an observational clinical study among 431 dental patients tongue status and blood pressure were documented.
[5] Those with an average home blood pressure ≥ 135 mmHg and/or ≥ 85 mmHg were referred to the primary health care centre, where three office blood pressure measurements were taken with one week intervals.
[6] Two independent blinded observers studied the photographs of the tongues.

Example ID: 26215326
QUESTION:
Does the clinical presentation of a prior preterm birth predict risk in a subsequent pregnancy?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] The objective of the study was to determine whether risk of recurrent preterm birth differs based on the clinical presentation of a prior spontaneous preterm birth (SPTB): advanced cervical dilatation (ACD), preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM), or preterm labor (PTL).
[3] Risks for sonographic short cervical length and recurrent SPTB were compared between women based on the clinical presentation of their prior preterm birth.
[2] Women were categorized based on the clinical presentation of their prior preterm delivery as having ACD, PPROM, or PTL.
[8] The lowest mean cervical length prior to 24 weeks was significantly shorter in patients with a history of advanced cervical dilation when compared with the other clinical presentations.
[7] The mean gestational age at delivery was significantly lower for those with a history of ACD (34.0 weeks) compared with women with prior PPROM (37.2 weeks) or PTL (37.0 weeks) (P = .001).
[1] This retrospective cohort study included singleton pregnancies from 2009 to 2014 complicated by a history of prior SPTB.
[6] Recurrent PTB occurred in 55.2% of patients with a history of ACD compared with 27.2% of those with PPROM and 32.2% with PTL (P = .001).
[5] Of 522 patients included in this study, 96 (18.4%) had prior ACD, 246 (47.1%) had prior PPROM, and 180 (34.5%) had prior PTL.
[4] Log-linear regression was used to control for confounders.

[2] We evaluated the accuracy of a duplex Doppler ultrasonographic index for predicting the presence or absence of esophageal varices in patients with compensated hepatic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A) by using endoscopy as the reference standard.

[0] Esophageal varices are present in 30% to 40% of patients in compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A) and in 60% to 85% of patients in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh classes B and C).

[9] For the 28 patients with SPI<5.0, the absence of esophageal varices in 27 of them could be correctly diagnosed using only SPI without invasive screening endoscopy, with high negative predictive value (96%) and sensitivity (96%).
[10] Of the remaining 28 patients with SPI ≥5.0, presence of esophageal varices could be similarly correctly diagnosed in 22 of them by using SPI without screening endoscopy, with high positive predictive value (79%) and specificity (82%).
[7] Mean PVV, SI, and SPI were all predictive of the presence of esophageal varices (p < 0.05) and SPI was found to be the most accurate parameter.
[8] Of the various cut-off levels of SPI evaluated, a cut-off value of SPI at 5.0, offered the highest diagnostic accuracy (88%).
[5] Univariate logistic regression analysis was followed by receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve construction for the indices that were significant.
[4] Mean portal vein velocity (PVV), splenic index (SI), splenoportal index (SPI), hepatic and splenic arterial resistive, and pulsatility indices (hepatic artery resistive index [HARI], hepatic artery pulsatility index [HAPI], splenic artery resistive index [SARI], splenic artery pulsatility index [SAPI]) were recorded.
[6] The indices HARI, HAPI, SARI, SAPI were not helpful (p > 0.05).

[1] However, the relationship appears to be more complex than often assumed and few studies have tracked it over longer time periods.

Did the call for boycott by the Catholic bishops affect the polio vaccination coverage in Kenya in 2015?

[2] In August 2015, the Catholic bishops in Kenya called for boycott of a polio vaccination campaign citing safety concerns with the polio vaccine.
[6] The national vaccination coverage was 93% compared to 94% in the November 2014 campaign.

[0] Polio eradication is now feasible after removal of Nigeria from the list of endemic countries and global reduction of cases of wild polio virus in 2015 by more than 80%.
[5] A total of 90,157 children and 37,732 parents/guardians were sampled to determine the vaccination coverage and reasons for missed vaccination.
[9] Compared to the November 2014 campaign, the proportion of children who were not vaccinated due to parent's refusal significantly increased from 6% to 12% in August 2015.
[8] Reasons for missed vaccination included house not being visited (44%), children not being at home at time of visit (38%), refusal by parents (12%), children being as leep (1%), and various other reasons (5%).
[4] A cross sectional survey was conducted in all the 32 counties that participated in the campaign.
[7] The proportion of parents/guardians that belonged to Catholic Church was 31% compared to 7% of the children who were missed.
[1] However, all countries must remain focused to achieve eradication.
